quiz.title: How HTTP Works
quiz.subtitle: From request methods to status codes and headers
quiz.description: Test your understanding of the HyperText Transfer Protocol — the foundation of data communication on the web.

options.text: HyperText Transfer Protocol
High Traffic Transfer Protocol
HyperText Transport Program
Host Text Transfer Protocol

questions.explanation: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol. It is the foundation of data communication on the Web, defining how messages are formatted and transmitted.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: There is no 'High Traffic' variant; HTTP is named for HyperText, not traffic patterns.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: It is a Protocol, not a Program, and Transfer is the correct word.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: The H stands for HyperText, referring to the type of content originally designed for — HTML pages.

questions.text: What is the default port number for HTTP?
questions.explanation: HTTP uses port 80 by default. Port 443 is used by HTTPS, port 8080 is a common alternative for development, and port 21 is used by FTP.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Port 443 is the default port for HTTPS (encrypted HTTP), not plain HTTP.
wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Port 8080 is commonly used as an alternative HTTP port in development environments, not the official default.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Port 21 is used by FTP (File Transfer Protocol), not HTTP.

questions.text: Which HTTP method is typically used to retrieve data from a server without modifying it?

options.text: POST
GET
PUT
DELETE

questions.explanation: GET is used to request data from a resource. It is safe (no side effects) and idempotent (multiple identical requests have the same effect as one).

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: POST is used to submit data to a server, often creating a new resource — it can modify server state.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: PUT is used to replace an existing resource with a new version.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: DELETE is used to remove a resource from the server.

questions.text: What does an HTTP 404 status code mean?

options.text: The server encountered an internal error
The request was successful
The client is not authorized
The requested resource was not found

questions.explanation: 404 Not Found means the server could not locate the requested resource. It is a client-side error, indicating the URL is wrong or the resource no longer exists.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: An internal server error is indicated by 500, not 404.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Lack of authorization is 401 (Unauthorized) or 403 (Forbidden), not 404.

questions.text: What does an HTTP 200 status code indicate?

options.text: The resource has been permanently moved
The request succeeded
The server is temporarily unavailable
The request is still being processed

questions.explanation: 200 OK is the standard success response. It means the request was received, understood, and the server is returning the requested resource.

wrongAnswerFeedback.D: A request still being processed returns 102 Processing or 202 Accepted, not 200.

questions.text: What is the key difference between HTTP/1.1 persistent connections and HTTP/2 multiplexing?

options.text: HTTP/1.1 keeps one connection open for multiple sequential requests; HTTP/2 sends multiple requests concurrently over a single connection
HTTP/1.1 uses UDP; HTTP/2 uses TCP
HTTP/2 requires a new TCP connection for each request; HTTP/1.1 reuses one connection
HTTP/1.1 multiplexes streams; HTTP/2 uses separate connections per resource

questions.explanation: HTTP/1.1 introduced persistent connections (keep-alive) so multiple requests can reuse one TCP connection — but they are still sequential (head-of-line blocking). HTTP/2 multiplexing allows many requests and responses to be interleaved concurrently over a single connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking at the HTTP layer.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Both HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 run over TCP, not UDP.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: This is backwards — HTTP/2 was designed to reduce connection overhead, not increase it.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: This reverses the two protocols — multiplexing is the HTTP/2 feature.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of the HTTP OPTIONS method?

options.text: To delete all resources under a path
To describe the communication options (allowed methods) for a target resource
To update a resource's metadata without changing its body
To retrieve only the response headers without the body

questions.explanation: The OPTIONS method asks the server which HTTP methods and headers are supported for a given URL. It is widely used in CORS preflight requests, where the browser asks the server if a cross-origin request is permitted before sending the actual request.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Deleting resources is the role of DELETE, not OPTIONS.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Updating metadata is done with PATCH or PUT.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Retrieving only headers is the purpose of the HEAD method, not OPTIONS.

questions.text: What is the difference between the HTTP PUT and PATCH methods?

options.text: PUT is idempotent and replaces the entire resource; PATCH applies a partial update
PUT appends data to a resource; PATCH replaces the entire resource
There is no difference — they are interchangeable aliases
PUT is used for binary files; PATCH is used for text files

questions.explanation: PUT replaces the complete target resource with the request payload, making it idempotent. PATCH applies a partial modification — only the specified fields are changed. PATCH may or may not be idempotent depending on implementation.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: This is reversed — PUT replaces the full resource; PATCH applies partial changes.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: They have distinct semantics defined in the HTTP specification (RFC 7231 and RFC 5789).
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Both methods are content-agnostic; the distinction is about full vs. partial replacement, not file type.

questions.text: What does the HTTP header `Cache-Control: no-store` instruct?

options.text: The response can be cached but must be revalidated before use
The response must not be stored in any cache
The cache entry expires after one second
The response is only cacheable by the browser, not by proxy servers

questions.explanation: `Cache-Control: no-store` tells every cache (browser, proxy, CDN) that it must not store any part of the response. This is the strongest directive for preventing caching, used for highly sensitive data like banking transactions.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Revalidation behavior is controlled by `no-cache` (must revalidate before use) or `must-revalidate`, not `no-store`.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: `no-store` has nothing to do with time-based expiry; use `max-age=0` for that.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Restricting caching to the browser only is done with `private`, not `no-store`.

questions.text: What does the `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` header indicate in an HTTP response?

options.text: The response body is compressed using gzip
The server will send the response in multiple TCP packets
The response body is sent in a series of chunks, allowing streaming before the full size is known
The client must split the request into several smaller requests

questions.explanation: Chunked transfer encoding allows the server to begin sending a response before it knows the total content length. The body is broken into chunks, each preceded by its size in hex. This is useful for streaming large responses or dynamically generated content.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Compression is indicated by `Content-Encoding: gzip`, not `Transfer-Encoding: chunked`.
wrongAnswerFeedback.B: All TCP responses involve multiple packets; chunked encoding is an HTTP-layer framing mechanism, not a TCP concept.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Chunked transfer encoding applies to the server response, not the client request.
